answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers: We selected articles describing the treatment of psoriasis with at least 1 biologic agent in the presence of a positive serologic or virologic marker for HBVor HCV before treatment onset that also had available serologic or virologic data on HBV or HCV at the end of follow-up. Any relevant study, regardless of design, was included. Literature reviews, expert opinions, and studies in which data of interest could not be extracted were excluded

question: Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers:
The systematic review of the literature included 49 studies comprising 312 patients followed for a mean of 30.9 months. Viral reactivation occurred in 2/175 patients who were seropositive for core antibody and 3/97 with HCV infection (yearly rates, 0.32% and 2.42%, respectively) compared with 8/40 patients with chronic HBV infection (yearly rate, 13.92%). 

The complete list of included studies appears in Appendix I (Supplemental Table I; available at  Table V13-60 summarizes the main characteristics of the included studies, and Table VI summarizes the reports describing viral reactivation during biologic therapy. Chronic HBV infection. In 40 patients, serology was compatible with chronic HBV infection. Mean follow-up time was 17.24 (range 0.75 39) months. On average, patients received 1.05 (range 1-2) different biologics. Viral reactivation was documented in 8 (20%) patients, and concurrent hepatitis in 1 patient, for a calculated yearly reactivation rate of 13.92%. Of the 26 patients with chronic HBV who received antiviral prophylaxis, 3 had viral reactivation, for an adjusted yearly reactivation rate of 7.74%, compared with 5/14 patients who did not receive prophylaxis, for an adjusted yearly reactivation rate of 26.31% (Supplemental Table II; available at  Resolved past HBV infection or core antibody only. Serology was positive for core antibody in 175 patients. Mean follow-up time was 42.66 (range 7.3-66) months. On average, patients received 1.4 (range 1-2.32) different biologics. Two patients received antiviral prophylaxis. Two patients (1.14%) had evidence of viral reactivation, for a yearly reactivation rate of 0.32%. None of the patients had hepatitis. HCV infection. Serology was suggestive of HCV infection in 97 patients (Table V).13-60 Mean followup time was 15.32 (range 3-46) months. On average, patients received 1.07 (range 1-3) different biologics. Evidence of viral reactivation was found in 3 patients (3.09%), for a yearly reactivation rate of 2.42% (Table VI). None of the 3 patients had evidence of hepatitis or received antiviral prophylaxis.

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
Our analysis has several limitations. First, different biologics, including various agents within the antieTNF-a family, were shown to confer disparate risk for serious infections among psoriasis patients.83 Nonetheless, we preemptively decided to pool data from heterogeneous studies evaluating different biologics to make possible an analysis of viral reactivation (which is an uncommon event) among a relatively small number of patients. Moreover, as the average follow-up period was limited, large-scale prospective trials evaluating distinct biologic therapies are in need to validate our conclusions. Second, we included case reports, which are known to have a positive-outcome publication bias.84,85 The case reports and small case series accounted for only 14.42% (45/312) of the total patients but 41.7% (5/12) of the patients with viral reactivation. Nonetheless, the possible overestimation of reactivation rates strengthens our conclusions regarding the safety
profile of biologic therapies.
